segments.s346-g89: همایون is used predicatively here as an adjective/noun meaning 'auspicious' or 'fortunate'. نباشد is a 3rd-person negative present/subjunctive/indicative (impersonal) meaning 'is not' or 'it is not so' and combines with چنین ('thus/so') to mean 'it is not so/it is not auspicious thus'. خود مگوی is an address to the second person: خود = 'yourself' (reflexive) and مگوی is the classical negative imperative 'do not say' (م- + گو + ی), so the second half gives an instruction: 'do not say (that) of yourself.'
segments.s346-g90: به is the preposition 'toward/into', taking راهی ('a path/way') as its object. که introduces a relative clause modifying راهی: هرگز نرفتی is past/perfect negative 'you never went' (second person), so 'a road which you have never traveled.' مپوی is the negative imperative addressed to the same addressee ('do not go / do not pursue'); placing the relative clause before the imperative is a normal classical Persian order.
segments.s346-g49: خردمند مهتر به گشتاسپ گفت is a simple past reporting clause: خردمند مهتر is a nominal phrase (an epithet: “the wise elder/leader”), به + گشتاسپ marks the indirect object/recipient (dative: “to Gushtasp”), and گفت is the past tense verb “said.” Word order is Subject (epithet) – dative phrase – verb, a common Persian narrative order.
segments.s346-g50: که introduces the reported speech (here a question). چندین is a poetic/modifying particle intensifying the question (here functioning roughly like “so/so much” or “how long/so long”), چه باشی is a subjunctive/questional form (باشی = second‑person subjunctive/optative of بودن used inside indirect question: “are you / be you”), تو is the emphatic/explicit 2nd‑person pronoun, اندر + نهفت is a locative/participial adjective meaning “hidden/within” (نهفت = hidden). Together they form the indirect question: “what / why are you hidden so?”
segments.s346-g51: برو is a second‑person singular imperative “go.” تا introduces purpose (“so that / in order that”) and governs the following subjunctive. مگر here is a modal/particle giving a sense of contingency or modesty (roughly “perhaps”/“maybe” in poetic usage). تاج و گاه مهی is the direct object of the following verb: تاج “crown,” و “and,” گاه مهی “the moon‑like (i.e. splendid) seat/time” (poetic epithet). The whole phrase sets the purpose: “Go so that perhaps you may see the crown and the moon‑like seat.”
segments.s346-g52: ببینی is the second‑person singular subjunctive/present used after تا to express the desired/purposed action (“you may see”). دِل+ت (دلت) is the possessed noun (“your heart”) formed with the enclitic -ت; گردَد is the present/subjunctive/pastive of گشتن here meaning “become.” از غم تهی is a prepositional phrase “empty of sorrow” (از = “of/from,” غم = “sorrow,” تهی = “empty”). Together: “may you see, and may your heart become empty of sorrow.”
segments.s346-g53: چو + بشنید is a temporal clause: چو = “when,” بشنید = past tense “he heard.” گشتاسپ is the subject; با او = prepositional phrase “with him.” برفت here is a poetic past form meaning “went away / departed” (verb of motion). The whole segment: “When Gushtasp heard, he went with him.”
segments.s346-g54: به ایوان قیصر is a prepositional phrase marking destination (“to the iwan/porch of Caesar”); خرامید is a past tense verb meaning “he strode/pranced proudly” (خرامیدن). The final token تفت in the line functions as a short poetic/climactic particle here and does not match the everyday verbal senses given in the glossary, so it is treated as a poetic particle or exclamation attached to the verb phrase. Together: “(and) he strode to Caesar’s iwan.”
segments.s346-g55: به marks motion/direction (“to/into”), پیغوله‌ای is a noun with the indefinitizing -ای meaning “a small ___” (in context a small pavilion/compartment or little conveyance — the glossary items given are ‘small message / little messenger’ but here the word denotes a small place or booth); شد فرود is a compound past construction where شد + فرود expresses ‘he alighted / he came down’ (فرود = descent). از مهان locates it relative to the nobles/guests (مهان = guests/nobles). The segment means: “He alighted into a small pavilion among the nobles.”
segments.s346-g56: پر + از + درد = “full of pain” (پر = full (of), از = of, درد = pain). بنشست is the past tense “he sat down” (بنشستن). خسته is a predicate adjective “weary/tired,” and نهان here qualifies the state as inward/hidden (either “hiddenly” or “inwardly”). The sequence is descriptive: “Full of pain he sat down, weary and inwardly (or secretly) afflicted.”
segments.s346-g33: بران is an adverbial locative ('thereupon, on that') referring back to the previous action. انجمن is the noun 'assembly' and is the direct object of بنشاندند; شاد here qualifies the manner ('joyfully'/'merry'). بنشاندند is a classical past transitive verb in the 3rd person plural ('they seated/placed'), so the phrase means 'thereupon they seated the assembly happily.'
segments.s346-g34: ازان پس is a temporal phrase ('after that'/'thereafter'). پری‌چهره is the definite animate object 'the fair‑faced (one)'; را is the Persian definite/direct object marker attaching to that noun. خواندند is the 3rd person plural past of خواندن used here in the sense 'to call/summon' (an extended sense), so the clause reads 'they summoned the fair‑faced woman.'
segments.s346-g35: کتایون is the proper name 'Katāyūn.' بشد is an archaic/simple past form meaning 'it came to be / she rose / she appeared' (here 'she arose/appeared'). با marks accompaniment ('with'), پرستار is 'nurse/caretaker,' and شست is the past form 'washed.' The clause therefore describes Katāyūn appearing and (with) the nurse washing (— implicitly, their hands).
segments.s346-g36: یکی دسته گل is a nominal phrase 'one bunch/a bouquet (of flowers).' هر یکی را with را marks 'each one' as the definite object of an implied verb; classical Persian frequently omits verbs that are understood from context. به دست literally 'into the hand' indicates the recipient: 'a bouquet into each one's hand' (i.e. 'they put a bouquet into each person's hand').
segments.s346-g37: همی گشت is a repeated/classical imperfective sense 'it kept turning / it continued to be.' چندان 'to that extent' intensifies what follows. کش آمد is a fixed/poetic compound here meaning 'came to be (in a burdensome/extended way)' and together with ستوه ('weary') yields 'she/it became so weary' or 'it grew so tiresome.' The phrase is idiomatic and somewhat compressed in classical style.
segments.s346-g38: پسندش نیامد is an idiom: پسندِش ('its liking/approval') + نیامد ('did not come/was not fitting') = 'it did not please.' کسی زان گروه means 'anyone/from that group'; with the negative implication here it reads 'no one of that company liked it.' The word order places the negative result before the specifying phrase.
segments.s346-g39: از ایوان سوی پرده is a sequence of prepositional/location words: 'from the iwan toward the curtain.' بنهاد is the past transitive 'he set/placed/turned' (classical usage), and روی is 'face'; together بنهاد روی means 'he turned/set his face (toward).' The clause describes a motion/attitude of turning one's face from the iwan toward the curtain.
segments.s346-g40: This is a string of adjectival/participial epithets describing the people who move: خرامان 'with a stately/proud gait,' و 'and,' پویان 'moving/active,' و 'and,' دل 'heart' + جفت‌جوی 'mate‑seeking' (a compound adjective/noun). Grammatically these are co-ordinated descriptors giving manner and inner state: 'stately and moving, with hearts seeking a mate.'
segments.s346-g9: This is a prepositional + noun phrase: ز کاخ پدر = “from (the) father’s palace,” with ز (a poetic variant of از) marking origin. دختر ماه‌روی is the head noun دختر (“daughter”) followed by the descriptive compound ماه‌روی (“moon‑faced/beautiful”), a typical Persian noun + modifier order.
segments.s346-g10: بگشتی is the narrative past meaning “(she) returned/turned.” بران is an adverbial/locative “to/onto that” referring to the previously mentioned assembly; انجمن is the noun “assembly.” جفت جوی is a compound: جفت “pair/mate” + جوی “seeking,” i.e. “seeking a mate.” The whole segment gives the verb + direction + purpose.
segments.s346-g11: پرستنده functions as a substantive ‘admirer/devotee’ (the subject); بودی is the past copula here (was/were). به گرد اندرش is a circumstantial phrase “around her,” where به گرد = “around” and اندر + ـش is the old-fashioned locative + possessive enclitic (“in/at her”). The phrase means “admirers were around her.”
segments.s346-g12: This line uses an inverted order. افسرش (her head‑ornament/helmet) is the item being spoken of, پدید is the verb “appear/be visible,” and نبودی is the negative past form meaning “was not.” ز مردم (“among/from the people”) sets the domain. Put together: “Her ornament did not appear among the people.”
segments.s346-g13: پس is a discourse adverb “then/so.” پرده قیصر literally “the emperor’s curtain” (i.e. the imperial harem/inner court) and آن روزگار “of that time/age” locates the statement historically. The whole phrase means “at that time in the emperor’s curtain/court.”
segments.s346-g14: سه (three, implied here) + بد + دختر forms the subject “three (bad/ill‑...) daughters/maidens”; classical Persian sometimes uses short adjectives before/after numerals and nouns. اندر جهان نامدار is a postpositional phrase “famous in the world” (اندر = in/within; نامدار = renowned). Note: the adjective بد is ambiguous in older usage; contextually the phrase names three notable maidens.
segments.s346-g15: This is a series of parallel adverbial/nominal qualities introduced by به “in/with”: به بالا “in loftiness/rank,” و دیدار “and (in) appearance/visage,” و آهستگی “and gentleness/softness.” The conjunction و links the qualities; the nouns describe attributes of the persons mentioned earlier.
segments.s346-g16: A parallel continuation: به بایستگی “with due propriety/fitness” (بایستگی is a normative noun) هم (also) به شایستگی “and with worthiness/merit.” The repeated به marks each quality; هم links the two parallel phrases.
segments.s346-g81: Literal order is Subject (تو) + with-phrase (با دخترت) + verb (گفتی) + object/command (انباز جوی). تو is the 2nd-person singular subject 'you'. با + noun (با دخترت) marks 'to/with your daughter' (indirect object/recipient). گفتی is a past-tense verb 'you said' (classical/past), here followed by the quoted/implied command. انباز is likely a proper name or uncertain archaic word used as the object; جوی is here a verbal noun/imperative sense 'seek, look for' (not 'stream') forming the command 'seek Anbâz.'
segments.s346-g82: نگفتی is a negative past 'you did not say' (negation of گفتی) introducing a contrasting clause. که is the subordinating particle 'that/which' linking to the clause 'رومی سرافراز جوی'. رومی سرافراز is a noun phrase 'the Rûm/Byzantine (ruler/man) who is lofty/triumphant', and the final جوی repeats the verbal sense 'seek' — so the clause states you did not tell her to seek the triumphant Roman.
segments.s346-g83: کنون is an adverb 'now/at present'. جست here is a past-form of جستن meaning 'sought' (not physical 'leap'); آنرا is the clitic object 'that (it)'; که introduces a relative clause; آمدش combines آمد + enclitic -ش 'it/he came' meaning 'it came to him' or 'it pleased him'; خوش is a predicate 'pleasant/pleasing.' Together: 'Now he sought that which pleased him' or 'Now he sought what pleased him.'
segments.s346-g84: This is an imperative admonition addressed to تو. از راه یزدان is a prepositional phrase 'from/by the way/path of God' (از = 'from' or 'for the sake of'), with یزدان = 'God'. سرت را is the object with the definite marker را ('your head'—literally your person/life). مکش is negative imperative 'do not draw/pull' → idiomatically 'do not risk/lose (your) head' (i.e. do not imperil your life).
segments.s346-g85: چنین بود is a copular phrase 'thus was' (چنین = 'thus/so', بود = past of 'to be'). رسم نیاکان تو is the subject/nominal phrase 'the custom/practice of your ancestors' (نیاکان = 'ancestors', تو is a possessive 'your'). Word order is typical Persian: predicate before the noun phrase when phrased as 'such was the custom of your ancestors.'
segments.s346-g86: A series of adjectival/nominative descriptors linked by و: سرافراز 'honored/triumphant', دین‌دار 'religious/pious', پاکان 'the pure (plural)'. The final تو reiterates possession/emphasis 'your (ancestors/them)'. The phrase qualifies the preceding 'رسم نیاکان تو'—describing the ancestors as honorable, pious, and pure.
segments.s346-g87: به آیین این is an adverbial phrase 'by/according to this custom' (به + noun phrase used adverbially). شد is the past of شدن 'became/it happened' and here introduces the resulting action. پی افگنده is a compound participial phrase meaning 'having set on pursuit' (پی = 'after/in pursuit of', افگنده = past participle 'cast/put on' — together 'set out after'). روم is the object/destination 'Rûm/Byzantium'. The clause says that, following that custom, (he) set off against Rûm.
segments.s346-g88: This is a direct admonition to تو: راهی مگیر is negative imperative 'do not take a road / do not set out' (مگیر = 'do not take'). اندر آباد بوم is a locative phrase 'into the inhabited/prosperous bōm (homeland/settled land)' — اندر = 'in/into', آباد = 'inhabited/prosperous', بوم = 'land/homeland'. So: 'you, do not take the road into the settled land.'
segments.s346-g65: چو is a temporal conjunction 'when' introducing a subordinate clause. دستور آموزگار آن is a noun phrase 'that instruction/order of the teacher' (دستور + the possessor آموزگار, with the demonstrative آن emphasizing that specific instruction). بدید is a past-tense, 3rd-person singular verb 'saw' placed at the end of the clause as is normal in Persian.
segments.s346-g66: هم اندر زمان is an adverbial phrase meaning roughly 'immediately / at the same time' (هم here stresses coincidence), with اندر زمان = 'in/at the moment'. پیش قیصر is a prepositional phrase 'before / in the presence of Caesar', where پیش takes the person as its object. دوید is the past 3sg 'ran / hastened' and is the main verb of this clause; the subject is omitted and understood from context.
segments.s346-g67: که is a connective/relative particle linking this clause to the previous. مردی گزین کرد is a past action 'chose a man' (مردی = 'a man', گزین is the verbal stem 'choose' and کرد is the past 3sg auxiliary forming the compound past). از انجمن marks source/selection 'from the assembly'. Word order is typical SOV with the verb ending the clause.
segments.s346-g68: به بالای indicates direction/destination 'to/onto the top of'. سرو سهی is the noun with its poetic epithet (سرو = 'cypress', سهی = a descriptive epithet meaning 'dark/tall' used poetically). در چمن is a locative phrase 'in the meadow' specifying where the cypress stands. The whole phrase describes where the chosen man was placed.
segments.s346-g69: به رخ introduces an attributive description 'with a face / in appearance', where به here marks the accompanying manner. چون گلستان is a simile 'like a rose-garden' (چون = 'like'). و با یال و کفت adds further descriptive features 'and with a mane and palms' (یال = 'mane', کفت = 'palm', a poetic image). These are a string of descriptive prepositional phrases modifying the person.
segments.s346-g70: که introduces a result/relative clause 'so that / such that'. هرکش is an indefinite pronoun 'whoever / anyone who'. ببیند (present/subjunctive form) is used generically 'sees', and بماند is the present-subjunctive/optative form 'remains / is left' (the prefixed ب- is the older Persian verbal prefix still used in classical style). شگفت is the predicative adjective 'astonished / amazed'. The clause therefore states the result of seeing.
segments.s346-g71: بد آنست is a fixed evaluation 'bad / unfortunate is that'. کو را is an archaic relative/indefinite construction meaning roughly 'whose / whom' (کو here functions as a relative pronoun with the object marker را). ندانیم is 1st-person-plural present 'we do not know' (classical negative form). کیست is a rhetorical interrogative 'who is (he)?' placed at the end for emphasis.
segments.s346-g72: تو گویی is an evaluative phrase 'you would say / one would say' (گویی here is an optative/second-person form meaning 'you might say' rather than the noun 'conversation'). همه modifies فره 'entirely / wholly', and فره ایزدیست is a nominal phrase 'divine glory' with the copula -ست: 'is divine glory' (ایزدیست = 'is godly / of the divine'). The clause expresses the impression one would get: that the person is all divine farr.
segments.s346-g41: هم آنگه is a temporal/emphatic phrase: هم adds immediacy and آنگه means 'then' ('just then'). زمین گشت is verb + subject (earth + past of گشتن) meaning 'the earth turned/ became.' چون introduces a simile ('like'), followed by پر زاغ ('a rook's feather/wing') as the likeness.
segments.s346-g42: چنین is a linking adverb 'thus/so.' تا introduces a result clause ('so that' / 'such that'). The clause has the inverted order سر از کوه (literally 'head/from the mountain') marking source, and the verb phrase بر زد (بر + زد) is a poetic past meaning 'struck up/appeared/rose up.' چراغ ('lamp/light') is the thing that 'rose up/appeared' at the end of the clause.
segments.s346-g43: بفرمود is a past honorific verb used here as 'he ordered/commanded/said.' قیصر is the agent ('Caesar') and the following که + prepositional phrase begins the content clause of that command: از کهتران marks source 'from the lesser/lowerranked ones.' The clause is elliptical and continues into the next segment.
segments.s346-g44: به روم اندرون is a destination phrase: به (towards/into) + روم (Rome/Byzantium) + اندرون (the inner quarters/within). مایه‌ور مهتران is a noun phrase modifying who is to be brought: مایه‌ور 'wealthy' + مهتران 'the great/chief ones' (plural). Together this completes the command begun in the previous segment.
segments.s346-g45: بیارند is a jussive/optative 3rd‑person plural form used to express the commanded action ('let them bring' / 'bring'). یکسر is an adverb modifying the verb meaning 'entirely' or 'all at once.' به کاخ بلند is a prepositional phrase 'into/to the lofty (high) palace.' Word order is verb phrase first (typical in poetry) followed by destination.
segments.s346-g46: بدان here functions as 'to that' (ب = to/into + دان ~ that/place) pointing to the palace; تا که introduces a purposive/result clause 'so that / in order that.' باشد is subjunctive/potential 'may be' or 'be (so)'; به خوبی پسند is an idiomatic predicate 'to good liking' = 'pleasing' (پسند as 'approval/pleasing'). The whole clause gives the purpose: they are brought so that it may be pleasing.
segments.s346-g47: چو is a temporal conjunction 'when/as soon as.' آگاهی آمد is a verbal phrase 'information/news came' (subject omitted or generic), and به هر مهتری is an indirect object/dative 'to each chief' (به marking the recipient). The phrase sets a time and recipient for the news.
segments.s346-g48: بهر is a preposition 'for/for the sake of' or 'concerning.' نامداری is the abstract 'renown/fame' and is coordinated with کنداوری by و. کنداوری here is a nominalized poetic word meaning deeds/valour/courage (parallel to 'fame'); the segment lists the subjects of the news (what the chiefs were being told about).
segments.s346-g25: Two parallel prepositional phrases joined by و (and). به بالای سرو = 'to/at the top of the cypress' (به + بالای, with بالای 'atop/above' governing the noun سرو). به دیدار ماه = 'toward seeing/the sight of the moon' (به + دیدار used as 'to behold' + ماه 'moon'). The و simply links the two locative/intentional phrases.
segments.s346-g26: نشستنش is the infinitive/masdar نشستن with the third‑person clitic -ش (= 'his/her/its sitting'), functioning as a noun ('her sitting'). چون introduces a simile/comparison ('as/like'). بر + سر گاه شاه is a prepositional phrase 'upon the (royal) seat of the king' — سر (literally 'top/head') and گاه ('place/occasion' here used poetically as part of 'سرگاه' = throne), with شاه as the possessor.
segments.s346-g27: یکی دسته = 'one/a certain group' (یکی as an indefinite/number modifier + دسته 'group'). دادی is the past form of دادن 'gave' (here a simple past transitive). کتایون follows as the agent/name (proper noun in apposition). بدوی is an epithet/qualifier attached to the name in this poetic line (an archaic/poetic adjective following the name).
segments.s346-g28: وزو is an archaic/poetic form (here functioning adverbially/locatively — 'to it' / 'upon it' in sense within the line) followed by بستدی, the past transitive 'fastened/closed' (بستدن). دسته is the direct object ('a band/group') and رنگ و بوی are two coordinated nouns ('color and scent') that qualify/describe that دسته. In syntax: بستدی takes 'دستهِ رنگ و بوی' as its object; و simply links رنگ and بوی.
segments.s346-g29: یکی here functions as an introductory 'one/once' (poetic indefinite) and انجمن کرد is a light-verb construction: انجمن 'assembly' + کرد 'made/did' = 'held an assembly'. قیصر بزرگ is the title/subject (Caesar the great) placed after the verb for poetic word order; بزرگ is an epithet modifying قیصر.
segments.s346-g30: هر آن کس که بودند introduces a relative clause 'whoever/each person who was (there)'; بودند is the past plural of 'to be' and marks existence/presence. گرد و سترگ is an adjectival/epithet phrase postposed after the clause — گرد here functioning as 'around/about' or 'present/nearby' in older usage and سترگ is a poetic epithet (here meaning 'great/illustrious' in context). The whole phrase picks out 'all those who were gathered and illustrious.'
segments.s346-g31: This is a temporal clause introduced by چون 'when/as'. به شبگیر is a poetic phrase referring to 'the nocturnal (thing/night)' (literally 'to/for the night‑visitor' used adverbially), and بردمید is an archaic past form meaning 'took away/carried off' — here the verb applies to آفتاب (the sun) as agent: آفتاب بردمید = 'the sun dispelled/took away [the night]'.
segments.s346-g32: سر نامداران = 'the heads/chiefs of the renowned (men)' — سر used as 'head/chief' with نامداران ('the famed/illustrious ones') in genitive relation. برآمد is the past 'arose' (intransitive), and ز خواب is a prepositional phrase 'from sleep' (ز + خواب). The sequence therefore says the leading men arose from sleep.
segments.s346-g1: چنان بود = 'was so/thus': چنان is an adverb meaning 'so' and بود is the past tense copula 'was'. قیصر is the subject placed after the verb (verb–subject order, common in older Persian) and بدانگه is a temporal adverb 'at that time/thereupon'. برای here introduces the reason or disposition that will be given in the following subordinate clause (it functions like 'for/so that').
segments.s346-g2: که introduces the subordinate clause ('that' / 'so that'); چون here functions temporally as 'when/as'. دختر او is the noun phrase 'his daughter' (دختر + enclitic pronoun او). رسیدی is the past verbal form 'arrived/was brought' (classical/poetic ending) and بجای means 'into place' or 'to the proper position' (i.e. having come to her place/role). The clause therefore reads 'that when his daughter arrived/in place...'.
segments.s346-g3: چو = 'when/as'. گشتی is from گشتن used here with a sense of 'having turned/being about' or 'wandering' (a poetic attribute); it modifies بلند اختر (literally 'high/tall star'), so the phrase pictures a 'high, roaming star'. و connects that phrase to جفت جوی, a compound meaning 'one who seeks a pair/a match' (جفت = mate, جوی = seeking). The whole phrase is an image describing the person who appears.
segments.s346-g4: بدیدی is the past of دیدن 'you saw' (2nd person past here, narrative/poetic usage). که introduces the content of that seeing ('that...'). آمدش is آمد + enclitic -ش 'to him/it' meaning 'came to him' or 'arrived for him'. هنگام is 'the time/moment', and شوی (from شدن/شو) is an infinitive/subjunctive sense 'to become / to take place'; together آمدش هنگام شوی expresses 'the time to become (be joined) had come for him.'
segments.s346-g5: یکی گرد کردی is a verbal phrase 'you gathered/assembled' (یکی here = 'together/one' or 'a certain', گرد کردی = 'made gather/assembled'). The verb is second person past کردی 'you did/made'. به کاخ انجمن is a locative/directional phrase 'at/to the palace (کاخ) an assembly (انجمن)', i.e. 'you assembled at the palace an assembly.' Word order places the adverbial/locative after the verb.
segments.s346-g6: بزرگان فرزانه و رای زن is a nominative list describing who was summoned: بزرگان فرزانه = 'the great, wise ones' (بزرگان plural noun + adjective فرزانه 'wise'). و links the next item; رای زن is a compound meaning 'those who give counsel' or 'women/people of counsel' (رای = opinion/counsel, زن here marks the agentive/participant — in context it reads 'counsel-givers' or 'counseling women/people').
segments.s346-g7: هرانکس که بودی is a conditional/relative phrase 'whoever he was' (هرانکس = 'whosoever', که بودی = 'that was' / 'whoever he may be' in narrative). مر او را places the dative and direct object together: مر = 'to me', او را = 'him' (definite object marker). همال is a noun meaning 'peer/companion' or literally 'carrier/one who bears alongside' here used as 'a match/equal'. The phrase therefore asks for 'his match/peer to be brought to me.'
segments.s346-g8: ازان = 'from those/of those' (pointing to the previously mentioned 'renowned' group). نامدارن = 'the famed/renowned ones' (plural). برآورده is the verbal form of برآوردن 'to bring up/produce'; in this imperative or instructive context it functions 'bring forth' or 'fetch out'. یال literally 'mane/crest' is a metaphorical attribute (raise the crest = bring forth the proud/esteemed man), so the phrase asks to fetch a peer from those famed ones, figuratively 'bring forth his plumed peer.'
segments.s346-g57: برفتند is a third‑person plural past verb 'they departed/went'. The following noun phrase بیدار دل بندگان is the (omitted) subject: a compound description where بیدار modifies دل ('hearts awake') and that whole phrase qualifies بندگان ('servants'), i.e. 'servants with awake hearts'. The verb precedes the subject here, a common poetic order in Persian.
segments.s346-g58: کتایون و گل رخ پرستندگان is a coordinated noun phrase: 'کتایون' (a proper name) and 'گل رخ پرستندگان' ('the worshippers/devotees of the rose‑faced [one]'). گل رخ is a two‑word attributive compound (literally 'flower‑cheek/rose‑face') describing a beautiful face. پرستندگان is the plural agent noun 'devotees/worshippers' and the whole phrase links back to the verb برفتند as additional subjects/participants.
segments.s346-g59: همی گشت is a continuous/habitual past sense ('he kept roaming/was walking'); همی marks ongoing or habitual action. بر گرد is a prepositional phrase 'around/about' and ایوان خویش is 'his own porch/portico' where خویش is the possessive 'his/own' (here split orthographically). The verb + prepositional phrase form the main predication 'he walked around his own portico.'
segments.s346-g60: پسش is an adverbial/relational word 'after him/behind him' indicating followers. بخردان و پرستار is a coordinated nominal phrase 'the wise (men) and the caretakers/attendants' (بخردان being plural adjectival/noun). پیش here functions adverbially ('before/in presence of' or 'in attendance'), so the whole segment says these people followed him (implicit copula/verb drawn from the previous clause).
segments.s346-g61: چو introduces a temporal subordinate clause 'when'. از دور is an adverbial phrase 'from afar/at a distance'. گشتاسپ را is the definite object marked by را (here the person seen). دید is the simple past 'saw', and گفت ('he said') follows, linking to the reported content clause. The sequence 'دید گفت' is a compact poetic coordination meaning 'on seeing (him), he said'.
segments.s346-g62: که introduces reported speech/content 'that'. آن خواب is the nominative subject 'that sleep/dream' and سر برکشید is a past tense verbal phrase literally 'raised its head' (an anthropomorphic idiom meaning 'became manifest/awoke'). از نهفت is a prepositional phrase 'from hiding/concealment' (از + نهفت), with نهفت functioning as a noun/participle 'hiddenness', so the clause reports that the sleep/dream emerged from concealment.
segments.s346-g63: بدان here functions adverbially ('with that' or 'thereby') — written as ب + آن in the text. مایه‌ور نامدار is a stacked adjectival phrase 'rich/wealthy and renowned' modifying the noun that follows. افسرش is 'his helmet' (افسر = helmet/crown plus possessive suffix ش = his). The whole phrase indicates he used that rich, famous ornament in relation to his helmet.
segments.s346-g64: هم‌آنگه is an adverb 'at that very moment/at once'. بیاراست is a past transitive verb 'he adorned/ arrayed' (third person). خرم سرش is a small adjectival phrase where خرم 'joyful/cheerful' qualifies سرش 'his head' (possessive suffix ش). In poetic order the adjective precedes the noun; the verb takes this phrase as its object/complement: 'he adorned (and) made his head joyful.'
segments.s346-g73: چنین داد پاسخ is a compact, somewhat inverted clause meaning “he gave such an answer / he answered thus”: چنین (adverb) modifies داد (past tense ‘gave’) with پاسخ as its object. The following که introduces a subordinate/wish clause; دختر مباد is a short optative wish where مباد is the negative desiderative (‘may it not be’), so that whole clause reads, “that (she) may not be a maiden/maidenly (of that sort).”
segments.s346-g74: که begins the relative/explanatory clause that modifies دختر. از پرده is a prepositional phrase ‘from/out of the veil’ (پرده = veil), followed by the object عیب 'fault/disgrace' and the verb آورد. Here آورد (from آوردن) functions as a stative/resultative verb meaning ‘bring about / cause’ in the sense required by the wish (مباد ... آورد) — i.e. “may she not bring/cause a disgrace.” بر نژاد is the locative/target phrase ‘upon the lineage.’
segments.s346-g75: اگر introduces a conditional. سپارم is first‑person singular present/subjunctive (classical/hortative use) of سپردن ‘to entrust/leave’, here carrying the hypothetical sense “were I to entrust / if I entrust.” من is the explicit subject (emphatic), بدو = به + او ‘to him’ (dative), and دخترم is the direct object with the possessive suffix ‘-م’ = ‘my daughter.’
segments.s346-g76: به ننگ is a prepositional phrase ‘to/with shame’ and اندرون specifies location ‘within / the inner apartments.’ پست گردد is an instance of poetic inversion: predicate (پست ‘low’) + verb گردد (گردیدن ‘to become’; here third‑person present/subjunctive/optative used as result clause), followed by سرم ‘my head’ (possessive clitic). Together the clause means literally ‘my head would become low with shame within the inner rooms.’
segments.s346-g77: هم او را places emphasis on the definite object (‘him also’); را is the definite/object marker. و آنرا که او برگزید is an appended coordinated object: آنرا ‘that one’ plus a relative clause که او برگزید ‘whom he chose’ (که introducing the relative, برگزید = past ‘chose’). The whole segment lists the objects to be punished: ‘both him and that one whom he selected.’
segments.s346-g78: به کاخ اندرون is a locative phrase ‘into/at the inner palace.’ The core verbal phrase uses the modal بباید (‘must/ought’) plus برید (the lexical verb ‘cut’/‘sever’); the object سر ‘head’ appears before the verb for emphasis (poetic inversion). Literal structure: ‘to the palace inside, the head must be cut’ = ‘their heads must be cut off inside the palace.’
segments.s346-g79: سقف گفت is subject + past verb ‘X said’; in context سقف is the speaker’s name. کاین نیست کاری گران is a copular/explanatory clause: کاین (‘that this’) + نیست (‘is not’) followed by کاری گران ‘a heavy / serious deed’ (کاری = affair/deed, گران = heavy/important). The whole means “he said, ‘this is not a serious/difficult matter.’”
segments.s346-g80: که here functions as a causal/explanatory conjunction ‘for/since.’ پیش از تو is the temporal phrase ‘before you’ (پیش + از + تو), بودند is the past plural of بودن ‘there were’, and چندی سران is an inverted noun phrase meaning ‘several chiefs/heads’ or ‘leaders for a while’ (چندی = some/several / for a while; سران = leaders). Together: ‘for before you there were, for a time, several leaders.’
segments.s346-g17: یکی بود is an existential past clause 'there was a certain/one'; مهتر کتایون is a noun phrase 'the chief (leader) of Katayun' where the usual ezafe is omitted for poetic compression; به نام introduces the following name/epithet ('by the name of').
segments.s346-g18: A string of coordinate epithets describing that person: خردمند 'wise' + روشن‌دل 'clear‑hearted' + شادکام 'fortunate'; the conjunction و links the adjectives and they follow the referenced noun (typical poetic order).
segments.s346-g19: کتایون is the subject; چنان دید 'saw thus/so' is a verbal phrase where چنان adverbially modifies دید; یک شب به خواب is an adverbial time/manner phrase 'one night, in a dream' (به + خواب = 'in sleep/in a dream').
segments.s346-g20: که introduces the content clause ('that ...') reporting the dream. روشن شدی is a past/resultative 'became bright' (shدی is a poetic/older past form of شد), and کشور follows in V–S order common in poetry; از آفتاب marks the source/agent of the brightness ('from/by the sun').
segments.s346-g21: یکی انجمن مرد is an indefinite noun phrase 'a certain/one assembly of men'; پیدا شدی is a compound meaning 'appeared/was found' (پیدا + شد indicating occurrence/coming into view), with poetic شدی as past marker.
segments.s346-g22: از انبوه مردم is a prepositional phrase 'out of the throng of people'; ثریا شدی literally 'became (like) Thurayya/Pleiades' — a poetic simile meaning someone stood out or shone among the crowd (ثریا used metaphorically); شدی again is the past 'became/appeared'.
segments.s346-g23: سر انجمن is a genitive/positional phrase 'head of the assembly' (سر = chief/head), with the verb بود 'was' following in past tense; بیگانه‌ای is indefinite 'a stranger' with the poetic -ی ending marking indefiniteness.
segments.s346-g24: A sequence of adjectival descriptions attached to that stranger: غریبی 'strange/odd', دل‌آزار 'heart‑aching/tormenting', و linking, and فرزانه‌ای 'a wise/sage‑like one' (فرزانه with the indefinite -ای). Adjectives follow the noun here, typical in poetic diction.

sentences.s346-s45: Don't call yourself so fortunate; don't go onto a road that you have never traveled.
sentences.s346-s25: The wise elder said to Gushtasp, "Why are you hiding so much?"
sentences.s346-s26: Go, that perhaps you may see the crown and the moon‑like seat, and your heart may be emptied of sorrow.
sentences.s346-s27: When Gushtasp heard, he went with him and strode toward Caesar’s iwan.
sentences.s346-s28: He alighted into a small pavilion among the nobles; full of pain, he sat down, weary and inwardly hidden.
sentences.s346-s17: Thereupon they joyfully seated the assembly, and then they summoned the fair‑faced woman.
sentences.s346-s18: Katāyūn arose; with her nurse she washed (her hands), and a single bouquet was placed into each one's hand.
sentences.s346-s19: It went on so that she became weary; nothing in that company pleased anyone.
sentences.s346-s20: From the iwan he turned his face toward the curtain — stately, moving, and with hearts seeking a mate.
sentences.s346-s5: The moon‑faced daughter returned from her father’s palace to that assembly, seeking a match.
sentences.s346-s6: Admirers gathered around her; among the people her head‑ornament was not visible.
sentences.s346-s7: Then, in the emperor’s curtain of that time, three maidens famed in the world.
sentences.s346-s8: In high rank and in appearance and in gentleness; with proper conduct and with worthiness.
sentences.s346-s41: You told your daughter, 'seek Anbâz'; you did not tell her to seek the proud Roman.
sentences.s346-s42: Now he sought that which pleased him; you, for God's sake, do not risk your head.
sentences.s346-s43: Such was the custom of your ancestors: your forebears were honorable, God‑fearing, and pure.
sentences.s346-s44: By this custom he set out in pursuit of Rûm; you, do not take the road into the prosperous homeland.
sentences.s346-s33: When he saw that instruction of the teacher, he at once ran into the presence of the Caesar.
sentences.s346-s34: They chose a man from the assembly and set him atop a tall dark cypress in the meadow.
sentences.s346-s35: With a face like a rose-garden and with a mane and palms, whoever sees him is left amazed.
sentences.s346-s36: It is unfortunate that we do not know who he is; you would say he is all divine glory.
sentences.s346-s21: Then the earth turned like a rook's feather, and thus a light sprang up from the summit of the mountain.
sentences.s346-s22: Caesar commanded that from the lesser men the wealthy chiefs be brought into the inner parts of Rome.
sentences.s346-s23: Bring them all at once into the lofty palace, to that place so that it may be well pleasing.
sentences.s346-s24: When the news came to each chief about the fame and the deeds of valour, ...
sentences.s346-s13: Her sitting atop the cypress and gazing at the moon was like sitting on the king's throne.
sentences.s346-s14: Katayun gave one group, and to it she fastened a band of color and scent.
sentences.s346-s15: The great Caesar held an assembly; everyone who was present and famed (i.e. all the great men) ...
sentences.s346-s16: When the sun rose and drove away the night, the heads of the renowned men rose from sleep.
sentences.s346-s1: The Caesar was, at that time, so disposed that when his daughter arrived in her place.
sentences.s346-s2: When the lofty, wandering star — a seeker of a match — appeared, you saw that the moment to be (joined) had come to him.
sentences.s346-s3: You gathered together an assembly in the palace of great, wise ones and women of counsel.
sentences.s346-s4: Whoever he may be, bring him to me as a match; fetch from among those renowned ones a peer (raise forth the plume).
sentences.s346-s29: Katayun's servants, whose hearts were awake, and the devotees of the rose‑faced one departed.
sentences.s346-s30: He kept walking about his own portico, followed by the wise men and the attendants.
sentences.s346-s31: When he saw Gushtasp from afar, he said that that sleep had lifted its head from concealment.
sentences.s346-s32: Thereupon, with that rich and renowned ornament he at once adorned his helmet and made his head joyful.
sentences.s346-s37: He answered thus: "May the maiden not be one who brings shame from behind the veil upon her lineage."
sentences.s346-s38: If I entrust my daughter to him, my head will be brought low with shame within the inner apartments.
sentences.s346-s39: Both him and that one whom he chose must have their heads cut off inside the palace.
sentences.s346-s40: Safaq said: "This is not a grave/hard task; before you there were, for a while, several chiefs."
sentences.s346-s9: There was a certain chief of Katayun named Khordmand, clear‑hearted and fortunate.
sentences.s346-s10: One night Katayun saw in a dream that the realm was brightened by the sun.
sentences.s346-s11: An assembly of men appeared; out of the crowded throng someone shone like the Pleiades.
sentences.s346-s12: At the head of the assembly there was a stranger — a singular, heart‑aching and wise man.
